journey.title: First circuit that made sense
journey.story: A small LED circuit made electricity feel less like a chapter and more like something I could reason about.
lessons: Every system needs a source, a path, and a reason to exist.
The simplest circuit can still teach cause and effect.
challenges: Understanding why current needs a complete loop.
breakthroughs: The moment the LED turned on made abstraction visible.

journey.title: Arduino became the bridge
journey.story: Microcontrollers connected code, pins, sensors, and visible behavior. It became a gentle doorway into embedded systems.
lessons: Software becomes more honest when it has to move hardware.
Debugging begins at the boundary between assumption and measurement.
challenges: Copying examples before fully understanding them.
breakthroughs: A blinking LED became a clocked signal I could control.

journey.title: Joined Electronics and Communication Engineering
journey.story: ECE gave the curiosity a direction: signals, circuits, digital logic, devices, and systems started joining into one map.
lessons: A subject becomes useful when I can place it inside a system.
Engineering is not a title. It is a way of tightening a mental model.
challenges: Connecting classroom theory to real artifacts.
breakthroughs: Digital electronics made hardware feel programmable.

journey.title: Started learning Verilog
journey.story: Verilog changed the question from writing instructions to describing hardware that exists across time.
lessons: Not every line of code is software.
Timing is part of the design, not decoration around it.
challenges: Thinking in registers and clocks instead of sequential programs.
Separating simulation behavior from synthesizable hardware.

journey.title: The FPGA board disagreed
journey.story: A simulation passed, but hardware exposed noisy inputs, timing assumptions, and an incomplete view of the real system.
lessons: Simulation is evidence under assumptions.
Hardware has no obligation to match a friendly testbench.
breakthroughs: Failure became a debugging map instead of a personal verdict.

journey.title: RTL and chip design became the direction
journey.story: The long-term goal became clearer: learn how digital ideas become reliable hardware and eventually silicon.
lessons: Direction appears through repeated attraction.
Documentation turns scattered learning into a usable system.
challenges: Finding the right depth for fundamentals.
Building proof of capability while still learning.
breakthroughs: The website itself became a learning archive, not only a portfolio.

failures.title: Simulation passed, board failed
failures.whatHappened: The ALU behaved correctly in ModelSim but produced unstable LED outputs on the FPGA board.
failures.whyItFailed: External inputs were treated as clean synchronous signals. The real switches bounced and crossed into clocked logic without conditioning.
lessons: Input conditioning matters.
A clean waveform can hide a missing assumption.
Synthesis reports are part of the design conversation.
failures.different: Synchronize and debounce external inputs before trusting them, then test with intentionally unfriendly timing.

failures.title: First mock VLSI interview exposed gaps
failures.whatHappened: Basic questions around timing, setup and hold, and Verilog semantics showed that recognition was not the same as understanding.
failures.whyItFailed: I had studied definitions without building enough examples where the concepts mattered.
lessons: Interviews reveal model depth.
Confidence should come from explanation and implementation, not familiarity.
failures.different: Prepare with small circuits, timing sketches, and spoken explanations instead of only notes.

failures.title: An overdesigned project dashboard
failures.whatHappened: I tried to build a big dashboard before the content system was clear.
failures.whyItFailed: The interface was solving presentation before the archive had stable data.
lessons: Architecture follows content.
A system should start with the smallest real workflow.
failures.different: Define the content model first, then build the interface around real entries.

versions.label: Curious child
changes: Collected small observations before knowing they were engineering habits.

versions.label: Discovered electronics
changes: Learned that small components can create visible behavior.

versions.label: Started building projects
changes: Learned that unfinished projects still teach architecture.

versions.label: Learning RTL
changes: Thinking in clocks, state, timing, synthesis, and verification.
Building toward chip design through documented practice.

configuration: Become capable of RTL and chip-design work through projects, notes, and fundamentals.
Digital design, Verilog, FPGA flows, timing, and VLSI fundamentals.
How an idea becomes gates, then timing paths, then silicon.
Depth, consistency, internship preparation, and choosing projects with real learning value.

knowledgeTree.title: Digital Design
knowledgeTree.description: The foundation for reasoning about logic, timing, state, and hardware behavior.
lessons: Boolean logic becomes useful when connected to constraints.
State machines are architecture in miniature.

knowledgeTree.title: Boolean Algebra
lessons: Simplification is not only math. It changes hardware shape.

knowledgeTree.title: FSM Design
knowledgeTree.description: A way to design behavior over time using states and transitions.
lessons: Naming states clearly makes debugging easier.
State encoding can affect synthesis results.

knowledgeTree.title: Verilog
knowledgeTree.description: A hardware description language for expressing synthesizable digital systems.
lessons: Simulation-only constructs can create false confidence.
Nonblocking assignments are part of thinking in clocked logic.

knowledgeTree.title: FPGA
knowledgeTree.description: A place where digital designs meet hardware reality before becoming silicon.
lessons: The board reveals assumptions that simulation did not include.

knowledgeTree.title: Timing Analysis
knowledgeTree.description: Understanding whether a design can reliably meet clocked behavior.
lessons: Timing is not a final report. It is a design constraint from the beginning.

knowledgeTree.title: RTL Design
knowledgeTree.description: Register-transfer thinking: data movement, control, state, and timing.
lessons: A good RTL block is understandable in both code and waveform.

currentlyBecoming: Clock-domain crossing, timing reports, RTL style, and VLSI fundamentals.
A living archive that makes learning visible instead of only listing outcomes.
Digital design notes, datasheets, and processor architecture material.
How to turn project work into proof of engineering thinking.
Choosing the right depth without freezing in research mode.
Document projects honestly, prepare for hardware internships, and build stronger fundamentals.
Small RTL blocks, better debug logs, and interactive archive systems.

dreams.title: Build a Brain in Silicon
dreams.why: Neuromorphic computing represents a fundamentally different approach to computation, inspired by how biological brains process information. The idea of creating hardware that can learn, adapt, and operate with extreme energy efficiency sits at the intersection of electronics, computer architecture, artificial intelligence, and neuroscience.
milestones: strengthen digital design and RTL fundamentals
complete advanced FPGA and hardware projects
learn computer architecture and processor design
study neural networks and machine learning fundamentals
understand spiking neural networks (SNNs)
explore neuromorphic hardware architectures
study research papers on brain-inspired computing
experiment with implementing neural models on FPGA
contribute to neuromorphic computing research projects
design and prototype a custom neuromorphic accelerator

dreams.title: Refine the Computational Architecture for Gaming and AI
dreams.why: Modern gaming and artificial intelligence push the limits of computation, memory systems, parallel processing, and hardware efficiency. Understanding and improving the architectures that power these workloads offers an opportunity to contribute to the future of high-performance computing, intelligent systems, and next-generation hardware platforms.
milestones: master digital design and RTL development
study computer architecture in depth
understand modern CPU microarchitecture
learn GPU architecture and parallel processing
study memory systems, caches, and interconnects
explore AI accelerator architectures
build processor and accelerator projects on FPGA
analyze performance bottlenecks in gaming workloads
study hardware-software co-design principles
research next-generation computational architectures
design custom architectures optimized for gaming and AI workloads

dreams.title: Become a Professor and Mentor Future Engineers
dreams.why: Engineering knowledge becomes most valuable when it is passed on. Beyond building systems and contributing to technology, I want to help shape the next generation of engineers by teaching, mentoring, and making complex concepts accessible. A career in academia would allow me to combine technical expertise, research, and education to create lasting impact beyond individual projects.
milestones: develop deep expertise in digital design and computer architecture
gain industry experience in RTL, VLSI, or chip design
contribute to significant engineering projects
build a strong portfolio of technical writing and educational content
mentor students and aspiring engineers
develop teaching and public speaking skills
pursue advanced studies and research opportunities
publish technical articles, papers, or learning resources
establish expertise within a specialized engineering domain
transition into teaching, research, and academic mentorship in my 40s

dreams.title: Create and Advance CENAQ
dreams.why: CENAQ represents a vision of bringing together Communication, Electronics, Nanotechnology, Artificial Intelligence, and Quantum Technology into a unified interdisciplinary domain. The goal is to explore how these fields converge to shape the next generation of computing, intelligent systems, advanced materials, communication networks, and emerging technologies that will define the future.
milestones: develop strong expertise in electronics and communication engineering
master digital design, RTL development, and computer architecture
study artificial intelligence and machine learning fundamentals
explore advanced communication systems and network architectures
learn nanotechnology fundamentals and nanoelectronics
study quantum computing principles and quantum information systems
identify convergence points between the five domains
publish frameworks and ideas defining the CENAQ domain
build interdisciplinary projects combining multiple CENAQ fields
establish a knowledge archive and research roadmap for CENAQ
grow CENAQ into a recognized framework for future technological innovation

butterflyEffect.title: One YouTube video
butterflyEffect.story: A small recommendation made electronics feel approachable.

butterflyEffect.title: Arduino
butterflyEffect.story: Code and hardware started talking to each other.

butterflyEffect.title: Electronics
butterflyEffect.story: The curiosity became a field of study.

butterflyEffect.title: ECE
butterflyEffect.story: Signals, devices, logic, and systems became connected.

butterflyEffect.title: RTL Design
butterflyEffect.story: The direction sharpened toward hardware that can become silicon.

butterflyEffect.title: Chip Design
butterflyEffect.story: The dream is still under construction.

letters.title: To the version still guessing
letters.letter: Keep building the small things. The project does not need to impress everyone to teach you something real.
letters.futureResponse: The small things became the map.

letters.title: To the version preparing for silicon
letters.letter: Do not confuse being early with being unqualified to begin. Keep making evidence.

whatChangedMe.title: Digital Design and Computer Architecture
whatChangedMe.description: The book helped connect gates, sequential logic, state machines, and computer architecture into one connected engineering map.
whatChangedMe.whyItChangedMe: It made digital design feel like a language for building systems instead of a set of isolated exam topics.
whatChangedMe.changedAfterward: I started treating Verilog, FSMs, ALUs, and timing as connected parts of the same direction.
whatChangedMe.impact: It pushed my interest toward RTL, processor architecture, and chip design.

whatChangedMe.title: The board disagreed with my simulation
whatChangedMe.description: The FPGA implementation failed even though the simulation looked correct.
whatChangedMe.whyItChangedMe: It showed me that engineering is not about proving my first idea right. It is about letting reality correct the model.
whatChangedMe.changedAfterward: I started reading synthesis warnings, thinking about inputs, and respecting timing assumptions.
whatChangedMe.impact: Failure became part of the documentation system instead of something to hide.

whatChangedMe.title: Working before engineering
whatChangedMe.description: Retail, customer service, and packaging work built habits around responsibility, communication, accuracy, and repeated process.
whatChangedMe.whyItChangedMe: It taught me that systems are not only technical. People, operations, documentation, and reliability matter too.
whatChangedMe.changedAfterward: I began noticing engineering patterns in ordinary work: constraints, workflows, feedback loops, and failure points.
whatChangedMe.impact: It shaped the way I think about discipline and systems.

whatChangedMe.title: A small electronics video
whatChangedMe.description: One video made electronics feel approachable enough to try instead of only admire.
whatChangedMe.whyItChangedMe: It lowered the barrier between watching and building.
whatChangedMe.changedAfterward: I moved toward Arduino, circuits, and eventually ECE.
whatChangedMe.impact: It became one of the small causes behind a much larger direction.

whatChangedMe.title: Mentor conversations about direction
whatChangedMe.description: Conversations around projects, internships, and technical depth helped me see that the path needs evidence, not only ambition.
whatChangedMe.whyItChangedMe: They turned vague interest into a clearer plan: build, document, explain, repeat.
whatChangedMe.changedAfterward: I started designing this site as a proof-of-learning archive.
whatChangedMe.impact: The archive became part of the growth system.

whatChangedMe.title: UART Transceiver in Verilog
whatChangedMe.description: A project that forced serial communication, timing, sampling, and clock-domain assumptions into a single concrete design.
whatChangedMe.whyItChangedMe: It made RTL feel like a disciplined way of thinking, not just another syntax to learn.
whatChangedMe.changedAfterward: I became more interested in synchronization, verification, and timing-aware design.

whatChangedMe.title: Choosing ECE
whatChangedMe.description: ECE gave my hardware curiosity a formal direction through electronics, communication systems, signals, digital logic, and devices.
whatChangedMe.whyItChangedMe: It gave me a field where the invisible layers of technology became worth studying seriously.
whatChangedMe.changedAfterward: I began connecting subjects into systems instead of seeing them as separate coursework.
whatChangedMe.impact: It is the academic root of the current RTL and chip-design direction.

replay.title: A circuit turns curiosity into evidence.
replay.summary: The beginning is not glamorous. It is the first time a simple system makes sense.

replay.title: Knowledge becomes a tree.
replay.summary: Concepts stop floating and start depending on each other.

replay.title: The board disagrees.
replay.summary: A failed build becomes a more accurate model of reality.

replay.title: Personal growth becomes version history.
replay.summary: The person building the projects is also being built.

replay.title: The dream points toward silicon.
replay.summary: The archive keeps moving because the engineer is still becoming.

decisionTrees.question: Why Verilog for the UART project?

nodes.label: Need to understand serial communication as hardware
Verilog exposes clocks, states, sampling, and synthesis
Software UART would hide too much of the timing behavior
Final decision: build synthesizable RTL and test on FPGA
Lesson: the technology should expose the concept being learned

notebook.title: Engineering note
notebook.note: The site has a normal reading mode and a notebook layer for process, uncertainty, and behind-the-scenes thinking.

notebook.title: Timeline annotation
notebook.note: Each milestone should preserve what changed in the mental model, not only what happened.

notebook.title: Failure annotation
notebook.note: A good failure entry names the assumption that broke.

notebook.title: Knowledge annotation
notebook.note: Prerequisites are signal paths through learning.

terminalCommands.current-status: Current status: learning RTL, documenting projects, preparing for hardware internships.
terminalCommands.future: Future direction: build reliable digital systems and contribute to silicon.
